metadata.chunk_id: Het einde van het algoritme als black box__a9-hybrid-small-to-big__0003
metadata.source_slug: Het einde van het algoritme als black box
metadata.source_title: The end of the algorithm as a black box
metadata.tags: Column, Technologie, Media & Telecom, Leisure, Inclusie
metadata.author: Julia Krauwer
metadata.description: The time when algorithms were accepted as a black box seems to be over for good. Government and society are calling ever more loudly for transparency. Algorithmic decision-making can in fact fuel inequality. The lawsuit that Uber drivers recently filed against the technology company illustrates this.
metadata.parent_text: Algorithmic decision-making can in fact fuel inequality. The lawsuit that Uber drivers recently filed against the technology company illustrates this. Companies no longer seem able to avoid showing how they use algorithms when making decisions. Taxi drivers demand transparency about how the Uber app works. The underlying algorithms determine a lot: they allocate rides and set the fare. In the background, the app combines all kinds of data to create a profile of the driver. It is unclear exactly what is recorded about the driver and how those data are used in algorithmic decision-making. To remove that asymmetry, drivers are invoking the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) in the Amsterdam court. “Whatever the ruling,” [writes Het Financieele Dagblad](https://fd.nl/ondernemen/1351533/baanbrekende-zaak-over-algoritmes-uber-waarschuwing-voor-andere-bedrijven), “companies that work with algorithms and artificial intelligence will prepare better for new transparency requirements because of this case.”. Can the algorithmic decision-making behind the Uber app be seen as disguised employment? That is one of the drivers’ questions. If the app is indeed an employer and not purely a platform, that would give Uber drivers a different position regarding their appraisal and pay. However, the drivers currently lack a fair basis to enforce a more balanced relationship. For example, they also worry that customers may unconsciously discriminate when they rate their driver.

To remove that asymmetry, drivers are invoking the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) in the Amsterdam court. “Whatever the ruling,” [writes Het Financieele Dagblad](https://fd.nl/ondernemen/1351533/baanbrekende-zaak-over-algoritmes-uber-waarschuwing-voor-andere-bedrijven), “companies that work with algorithms and artificial intelligence will prepare better for new transparency requirements because of this case.”. Can the algorithmic decision-making behind the Uber app be seen as disguised employment? That is one of the drivers’ questions. If the app is indeed an employer and not purely a platform, that would give Uber drivers a different position regarding their appraisal and pay. However, the drivers currently lack a fair basis to enforce a more balanced relationship. For example, they also worry that customers may unconsciously discriminate when they rate their driver.

text: It is therefore not surprising that artificial intelligence as a theme is being approached ever more critically. metadata.source_slug: Het einde van het algoritme als black box

metadata.tags: Column, Technologie, Media & Telecom, Leisure, Inclusie
metadata.author: Julia Krauwer
metadata.description: The time when algorithms were accepted as a black box seems to be over for good. Government and society are calling ever more loudly for transparency. Algorithmic decision-making can in fact fuel inequality. The lawsuit that Uber drivers recently filed against the technology company illustrates this.
metadata.parent_text:
Tech giants such as Google and IBM are already sharing their knowledge on this topic more widely. They provide extensive documentation on how they counter bias in algorithms and make tools for software developers available free of charge. Given the potential injustices that can flow from algorithms and the increased social attention to them, this trend towards self-regulation will only strengthen. It is time for companies – large and small – to look more critically at how they use algorithms to make decisions and how they communicate about this with their customers. In the future, companies’ competitive advantage may lie increasingly in transparency about the algorithm, and less in the algorithm itself. *This article previously appeared at [Emerce](http://www.emerce.nl/).*
About the author. [Julia Krauwer](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/auteurs/julia-krauwer.html "Over de auteur - Julia Krauwer") is sector banker TMT (Technology, Media & Telecom) at ABN AMRO. She works daily on the impact of technology on business models. Read more in the technology sector. Technology, Media & Telecom (TMT) is one of the fastest-growing sectors. Helped by the increasing use of software, algorithms and sensors, this sector is at the basis of the digital renewal of other sectors.
